watching the whole season of a show. Start to finish. Eyes glazed. No food. Started eight in the morning. Keep going until it's done. Really? How many hours of video did you say 12? Oh, but, but I won't watch more than seven minutes on YouTube. Well, that's because you're not interested in what you're watching on YouTube. Nobody has attention span, except for things that they find interesting or entertaining or personally relevant. And for that people have virtually unlimited attention span. This has never happened. There's a first time for everything, baby. Yeah, you're right. I should go to Vegas tonight. Get on the plane. So this is a, this is from a FBI special agent. Here's his formula. Proximity plus frequency plus constancy or duration plus intensity equals friendship. So by proximity, what he means is close, like physical, ideally, that's why it's still hard to beat selling in a room, a real room, floor, four walls, people like humans like you in chairs, with tables or without. It's still hard to beat that with any kind of distance media. Very hard to beat it. The problem is the distance. That's, that's the problem. Proximity matters. That doesn't mean we shouldn't use media. It doesn't mean we don't want to use media, et cetera, et cetera. But the degree of difficulty is really much higher. Frequency, well, we know, this is, this has never changed. The number of contacts we have with a person in a, in a quick succession matters. And people have rules about this. Internal embedded rules. All women of a certain age had the three date rule. Hence the old joke, bring her home, drop her off, got their car, go back up, bring the doorbell, I'm here for number two. Right? Cuz, cuz there's a three date rule. And that's built in. Mama, I guess, taught it. I don't know, but it's there. I mean, it's there. Right? So frequency matters. That's why if we do a three step campaign, they're no more than 15 days apart. Not three months apart. And if we can fill them in with email in between, so much the better. The more frequently we show up, the quicker and closer we get to familiarity, which moves us to trust lower's defenses. Constancy or duration, total time spent with. Important. Read Neil Strauss' book, the game, look at what the FBI knows. You can find it, you can find my formula everywhere. Time obtained by either duration of time. The 15 minute conversation at the bar becomes removed to four bars together. And we spend the rest of the evening together. Duration or constancy, same bar, see each other, tonight, tomorrow, every Friday. Every Friday, you're there, I'm there. Constancy, one of those two things, or both of them added together. A lot closer to you saying, why not? That's her name, people, that's her name. So total time, it's still the best, it's like the best. It's just the best. Just the best, every woman should change her name, why not? I just, if I write a novel and there's a stripper in it, I'm telling you, it's gonna be Tiffany, why not? It just, you couldn't be better. Intensity, so what does intensity really mean, right? How closely you message to market match, that's what it means. How closely you reinforce and align with their preexisting beliefs. How much affinity there is. All of these things, if they fire on all cylinders, that's intensity. So we've all had the experience, right? You meet somebody and like real quick, you're like Sympatico. I mean, you are like, this is just, we just are like, whether buddy or non, it's a match made in heaven. My God, I can't believe, we like the same music, we read the same books, we have the same political opinions, we have the same, right? And it's like firing on all cylinders, right? Well, when you create that, you get to an outcome you want real quick. So those are the things in his formula. And they simply make the argument for time, and even greater argument. So when you let anybody convince you, persuade you, that you ought to sacrifice time in communication, influence, persuasion, any of that, you are buying a completely erroneous argument. You're buying something that does not, now. So this morning when the word efficient was used, you used it in your question. So, you know, Peter Drucker said, the management guru said, there's a big difference between efficiency and effectiveness, and it's important to know the difference. Ideally, we like to be both at the same time. Nothing beats being effective and efficient. But more often than not, we have to choose one or the other, right? Like my high school aptitude test, I was supposed to be a social worker. Swear to God. Swear to God, okay? And I didn't cheat. I mean, I didn't have somebody else take the test. I took the test. And profoundly, I'm supposed to be a social worker. Now, I would be an efficient social worker. Right? Stop, get a job, get out. Next, right? I mean, I'd move them through, baby. But I don't know that I'd be that effective as a social worker. You know? I don't know. So you don't want to trade away effectiveness for efficiency at any time. But particularly when there's money at stake. So anybody who is trying to bully you into trading away effectiveness for efficiency, there is a better answer to whatever problem that case is being made as a solution for. There's a better answer. Because a person who's really interested in making a luxury car choice at that price point, 90 minutes is no big thing. So part of the answer is often get to the right person. Don't compromise the presentation for the wrong person. So if you have no interest in something, almost any amount of time is too long. What would be an appropriate amount of time if you have no interest in something? How much time would you like to give it? Well, I think the answer is none, right? I mean, so every minute we get from you is, so there's an old sales axiom, right? Old style sales management says it's easier to give a presentation to a lot of people than it is to find just the right person to give a presentation to. That's old sales management. That's this is a numbers game. Go out, start the story with as many people as possible. That's the deal. Our whole approach is it's better to find somebody who wants to hear the sales presentation and then you deliver the best sales presentation that you possibly can. That's a choice that you make. And this time thing is really, really important. I think I'm going to skip because you are well aware of it. A few other things I could have said about it, but I'm not. So I'm going to skip membership concept and we're going to look at. We're going to look at hijack 60. So on your page 89 is another great Eric Hoffer quote. And the first four words are really pretty good. Five or six, whatever it is, glory is largely a theatrical concept. So when I talked about before in the Fernandez practice management deal, grown people, doctors coming up on stage kneeling down in front of the throne to be knighted with the sword. So that's glory is a theatrical concept, right? And he's right. There is no striving for glory without a vivid awareness of an audience. The knowledge that one's meritorious thing is going to be seen by somebody. And many people do this in make believe environments. So I have a good friend who's a big avid golfer. And his life is about winning the tournament at the local club. Well, this is a completely artificial construct. First of all, there's no compensation. There's cost, but there's no money in it. This is like to me, horse shows versus horse racing. So high school I dated a girl with horse shows. You get ribbons. That's what you get ribbons in a closed sorority, in a closed environment. Everybody gives everybody else ribbons. It's massive expense, by the way. I mean big, but it's fake. But it satisfies enormously because there's an audience. There's recognition. There is glory. There's a winner and a second place finisher and a third place finisher. Dog shows, same deal. Nobody gives a rat's ass except the other dog owners. They're all getting together in a circle, looking at each other and handing out ribbons. There's no money. When we race horses, we actually, we still lose money, but I mean, we get money. We don't get as much as we spend nine out of ten times. But I mean, the quickest way to get rich in the horse racing business, start a lot richer than you intend to wind up. That's how you get rich in the horse racing. But so there's all these kind of artificial environments where glory drives everything. That if you removed the audience, nobody would try and do any of it. So if you're going to ride a horse for pleasure, you know what you do? You go ride a horse for pleasure. You're out in the woods. You ride around. You have a good time. Nobody would get in a ring on a horse, jump over fences, teach it to jump over fences, fall off, break their nose, on and on and on and on and on. If nobody watched and nobody applauded and nobody gave out ribbons. It wouldn't even exist. People would get on horses and go ride around in the woods and have a nice time. Nobody would fish in a tournament if there was no audience and there were no trophies. They'd just go fish. So there's a crap load of things we do. Only because there is the theatrical concept of glory. And it's very motivational even to people who make a lot of money and otherwise shouldn't need it. They think this is serious. And I don't know how that's even possible. But it is and it happens. Everybody is susceptible to this. Everybody. Everybody wants to be in an environment where glory occurs as a theatrical concept. If you create it for a group of people, they will reward you nicely for what it is that you do. I gave you a couple articles. The one on page 90 is all about every baby can be a model. So moms have a baby in one arm and their iPhone or iPad in another. Says the global director of integrated media for huggies. Honest to God. Who would even imagine? There's a global director of integrated media for a diaper company. Really? Yep, there is. And so this is all about user generated content is a lower cost way to feed the growing beast of online marketing. Everybody wants the pictures of their baby up there to be famous for 15 minutes. Warhol was right. He just didn't know it would be monetized as much as it is. So kind of tied to this then is activity masquerading as achievement. Which is another kind of closed environment thing. So it's best first of all if there's a small victory process. If there's something people can do with your product or with your service or in your environment from which they get a victory pretty quickly not having to do much. And so the way we fail them is on page 93. So we fail them by giving them too big a task to do too soon. So if you sell get rich at real estate and their first victory is a deal big problem. If you sell weight loss and their first victory is losing 10 pounds big problem. So we fail them by giving them too big of a task to do too soon. Secondly too hard of a task to do too soon. The best thing for them to do immediately is small and easy. When I built the original magnetic marketing system one of my big premises was how can I put a bunch of stuff in here. It's easy to do. It gets a quick result and they can't screw it up. If they do it it's impossible for them not to get a result. So like the three step lost customer mailing. It's impossible. Somebody. Now you may not make a profit. You may mail a thousand amount and only one person comes in. But see that's activity versus accomplishment. People don't care. The very fact that something happened because they thought nothing was going to happen. So something happened. They're excited. Well you cannot send out the basic three step lost customer campaign that's in magnetic marketing to lost customers of any business and I don't care how big a screw up you were when you lost them. Somebody's going to forgive you as a result of those three steps and respond to the offer. That was my whole premise was small. Easy. Can't mess it up. Creating too long of a delay for victory. That's the third thing. So they got to get a victory quick. So we shit like with proactive. You got to ship the thing that that makes one go away right now. If all you do is send them the stuff that solves the problem over like six weeks and then keeps it under control forever. You are not going to get to six weeks. It's not going to happen. Failing to create a progressive series of victories. One has to lead to another to another to another. That lead them to their own devices. Having too long a delay for reward and recognition. Monthly sales contest if you're managing sales people. El no good. Daily. Daily. If not hourly if you got a bunch of them. You can't let them wait too long before they get a pin. And not converting victory to commitment to the next harder task. The time to get commitment for the next thing you want them to do is right after they've gotten a result from the thing you want them to do. So my very first business experiences were in Amway and I learned a lot about a lot of things. I'm going to tell you another one in a minute. But the very first thing I understood quickly was the minute I signed somebody up they were trying to quit. That's exactly what they were trying to do. My ass wasn't out the door. And John and Martha quit. That's what they're doing. Now what we were taught was stupid. It took me a little longer to figure this out but I got there. So we were taught if you've ever been in an MLM you were taught the same thing because there's five different businesses. There's the business the company's in. They're in the same business. The distributors not by any stretch of the imagination. It's not a criticism by the way. It's neither here nor there. It just is what it is. So we were taught if you sponsor somebody you immediately get a Christmas card list. You book two living room meetings usually if you got them on Thursday and Tuesday or Tuesday and Thursday they invite all their friends lying to them over to the meeting and you show the plan and you help them recruit distributors. Now this is important by the way for a reason I'll get to in a second but here's the problem that I figured out immediately. If you're amway that's exactly what you want everybody doing because they don't care about individually who wins and who loses. They just care about the overall multiplying effect of having a bazillion people doing this every week. You however who only have him maybe you got him and you got them. That's all you got. You care deeply about whether they quit or not. If you do what they teach you most people have immediate defeats. They don't have immediate because a quite commonly you are there at their house for their Thursday night meeting that they have invited their friends to and either lay.
